expectations.text: A criterion benchmark file is produced with benchmarks at multiple dataset sizes (at least small n~10 AND large n~200)
expectations.evidence: bench_overlap.rs has bench_typical_usage (n=12), bench_realistic_scaling (n=10,15,50,100,200), and bench_worst_case_scaling (n=10,15,50,100,200).

expectations.text: At least 2 implementations are compared (current pairwise vs sort-then-scan or similar)
expectations.evidence: alternatives.rs contains 3 implementations: detectar_solapamientos_original, detectar_solapamientos_sort_scan, detectar_solapamientos_sort_partition.

expectations.text: The benchmark uses BenchmarkId or similar to parameterize by size for scaling analysis
expectations.evidence: bench_realistic_scaling uses `BenchmarkId::new("original_n2", size)` and `BenchmarkId::new("sort_scan", size)` parameterized over [10, 15, 50, 100, 200].

expectations.text: The output acknowledges that the winner may differ by dataset size (crossover point analysis)
expectations.evidence: report.md states: 'At n≤15: negligible difference, no harm adopting the optimized version. At n=200 (bulk import): expected 3-10x speedup.' Includes a table showing different winners at different sizes.

expectations.text: The benchmark generates realistic contract data (proper date ranges, mixed propiedades)
expectations.evidence: generate_contratos uses multiple propiedades (n/5), 70% active/20% vencido/10% cancelado, date ranges spanning 12 months with 90-365 day durations. Also has generate_worst_case for stress testing.

expectations.text: A clear recommendation is made that accounts for BOTH the normal case (n=5-15) and bulk import case (n=200)
expectations.evidence: report.md recommends sort_partition: 'At n=10-15: Performance difference is negligible... At n=200: Sort+partition avoids the HashMap allocation overhead... No downside at small n.'
